# Dokkimi

> Dokkimi is a testing framework for backend engineers, platform engineers, QA teams, and AI-native development teams. It runs integration, E2E UI, visual regression, and database tests in isolated Docker environments — without requiring a staging environment or code changes to your services. Built to integrate natively with AI coding agents like Claude, Cursor, and Copilot.

Dokkimi is for engineers frustrated with the gap between unit tests (which mock away the interesting parts) and staging environments (which drift from reality and cost too much to maintain). Each test run gets its own Docker network with dedicated services, databases, and a headless browser — no shared state, no cross-test interference. Tests are defined declaratively in a `.dokkimi/` folder and executed via the `dokkimi` CLI.

Key capabilities:
- Isolated per-run Docker networks with dedicated services and databases
- E2E UI testing with headless Chromium browser automation
- Visual regression testing with screenshot diffing against baselines
- Interceptor sidecars that capture every HTTP request between services for assertion
- Mocking of outbound calls to third-party APIs (Stripe, Auth0, Twilio, and more)
- Database seeding for Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, and Redis
- Parallel step and test execution
- Zero code changes to services — sidecars, routing, DNS, and cleanup handled automatically
- AI agent integration — test definitions in YAML/JSON that agents can read, write, and iterate on

Install: `npm install -g dokkimi` or `brew install dokkimi/tap/dokkimi`. Requires Node.js 20+ and Docker.

## Docs

- [Getting Started](https://dokkimi.com/docs): install, prerequisites, scaffold, and run your first test
- [Project Structure](https://dokkimi.com/docs/project-structure): layout of the `.dokkimi/` folder
- [Services](https://dokkimi.com/docs/services): defining the services that make up a test topology
- [Databases](https://dokkimi.com/docs/databases): Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis seeding
- [Tests and Steps](https://dokkimi.com/docs/tests-and-steps): structuring test definitions and step groups
- [Actions](https://dokkimi.com/docs/actions): HTTP calls, UI steps, DB queries, and more
- [Assertions](https://dokkimi.com/docs/assertions): asserting on traffic, responses, headers, logs, and DB state
- [Variables](https://dokkimi.com/docs/variables): hardcoded variables and runtime extraction with `{{variable}}` interpolation
- [Mocks](https://dokkimi.com/docs/mocks): intercepting outbound calls to external APIs
- [Refs](https://dokkimi.com/docs/refs): referencing shared definitions across files
- [CLI Reference](https://dokkimi.com/docs/cli): all `dokkimi` commands (`run`, `init`, `doctor`, `inspect`, ...)
- [AI Integration](https://dokkimi.com/docs/ai-integration): using Dokkimi with AI coding assistants

## Tutorials

- [Mocking OAuth Flows](https://dokkimi.com/tutorials/mocking-oauth-flows): test Auth0 and other OAuth providers without test tenants
- [Testing Next.js Apps](https://dokkimi.com/tutorials/testing-nextjs-apps): end-to-end testing a Next.js application
- [Testing External API Integrations](https://dokkimi.com/tutorials/testing-external-api-integrations): mock Stripe, Twilio, and other third-party services
- [Testing LLM Integrations](https://dokkimi.com/tutorials/testing-llm-integrations): test OpenAI and other LLM API integrations

## Blog

- [Why we built Dokkimi](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/why-we-built-dokkimi): the motivation behind the project
- [Getting started with Dokkimi](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/getting-started-with-dokkimi): walkthrough of a first test
- [How traffic interception works](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/how-traffic-interception-works): the interceptor sidecar architecture
- [Mocking external APIs](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/mocking-external-apis): intercepting calls to Stripe, Twilio, and more
- [Testing with real databases](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/testing-with-real-databases): seeding and querying Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB
- [Parallel test execution](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/parallel-test-execution): running tests concurrently
- [Reusable definitions with $ref](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/ref-pattern-reusable-definitions): the $ref pattern for shared configs
- [Debugging failed tests](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/debugging-failed-tests): reading output and fixing failures
- [Variables and extraction](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/variables-and-extraction): using runtime variables in test steps
- [Console log assertions](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/console-log-assertions): asserting on service console output
- [Dokkimi vs Docker Compose testing](https://dokkimi.com/blogs/dokkimi-vs-docker-compose-testing): why Docker Compose alone isn't enough
